data.title: UnitedHealth: Why It's Still Not Too Late To Buy The Turnaround
data.description:
UnitedHealth: 2026 Should Be the Reset Year

Is the worst really over for investors of UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH)?

At this point, UNH has become a true battleground stock in the healthcare sector.

The integrated healthcare leader is coming under such immense pressure across its business segments. And visibility remains relatively limited at this point.

Regulatory challenges continue to hamper any sort of near-term catalysts to drive a more sustained rebound.

Therefore, it is likely to limit any aggressive buying accumulation in the next few months, or at least until UNH reports more constructive earnings for H1 of 2026.

Although I'm somewhat ambivalent about whether we will get enough information from Q1 to show us that the recovery initiatives are indeed panning out.

Plus, based on a recent UnitedHealth management conference in March, I think the key takeaways are that it's still far too early for the company to provide us the key insights that we need, indicating that 2026 should still be a reset year to ruminate on.

In late January, I decided to stay put with my Buy rating on UNH. While I noted that sentiments were decidedly fragile, I was confident that the market had already priced in substantive policy risks into its valuation.

UnitedHealth: Earnings Prospects Should Improve From Here

After all, Wall Street doesn't expect things to get worse from UNH's earnings perspective.

I think its dominant position in the Medicare Advantage market should help it adjust its pricing levels to be more attuned to the changes in the CMS reimbursement rates.

So it could still make alterations to its coverage levels and benefits schedule.

Also, the company could still exit markets that may no longer make much sense in the current environment where the market is no longer pricing it for growth against peers.

In other words, right now expectations are so low, I believe the market is looking at management executing a cohesive and reasonably baseline plan to help the earnings trajectory bottom out according to the provided guidance.

Right now, I guess investors want to know that the margin levels should be able to hold firm.

Obviously, UnitedHealth has to get its act together while navigating the headwinds in increasingly higher medical cost ratios, which the company warned about in its annual filings.

But these are already known risk factors that have played out in the market.

I think it's fair for me to say that unless things worsen further from the rates disappointment that we already faced in the advance notice, things shouldn't be too bad from here, right?

In other words, once we get past this noteworthy announcement, I think we should be able to see buyers coming back more constructively.

Over the weekend, it looks like UNH bulls can finally get some respite and much-needed relief.

Accordingly, the CMS "has finalized a rule to eliminate nearly a dozen metrics in the Medicare Advantage Star Ratings system."

By doing that, it aims to "provide health insurers with more than $18B over the next decade."

Things are starting to look on the bright side of the ledger for UNH, and I think it portends well as we look forward to the final notice later this month.

Fingers crossed on that one.

While we may have to rein in our expectations of bigger rate increases from 2027, I don't think the market is delusional about it, given the valuation compression that has already occurred, right?

Also, recall that I mentioned earlier that the company has several levers that could help its margins inflect back up?

Management reminds us that these levers don't just apply to its insurance business.

In fact, because of its tight integration, the margin improvement opportunities for the Optum-related segments should also benefit, aligning with UNH's "integrated value-based care" business model.

And because of the company's scale, I guess it could positively influence contractual negotiations with customers accordingly and not be compelled to take on businesses that may no longer quite justify a slower-growth environment.

I think we should expect these changes to play out throughout the whole of 2026 and potentially through 2027 as well.

However, we should afford management the benefit of the doubt to show us that the growth levers across the various segments can help the margin turn back up northwards, as explained in the recent Barclays 28th Annual Global Healthcare Conference:

Of course, we don't have to simply take management's word for it.

I guess that's why we have projections that we could rely on, helping us to ascertain the execution while we consider the gap between what were its previously more "typical" 13% to 16% adjusted EPS growth targets.

From what I gleaned, the next two years (CAGR of 10.4%) will see UnitedHealth's earnings growth remain below its previous long-term trend.

So, I wouldn't expect management to return in a big way to surprise us to the upside. But, my point is that the bar is set quite low for the company to give us a pleasant surprise, in any case.

However, the challenges are aplenty (regulatory challenges, medical costs creeping up, pricing adjustments, etc.). It's a mixed bag, you know what I mean.

UNH: Valuation Discount Reflects Risks Accordingly

And if we were to take a cursory glance at UNH's valuation (15.5x forward P/E), it is clear that it remains well below the 10-year average of almost 19x.

But, let's be honest about it.

Do you really think that until UNH's forward earnings can track closer to its previous long-term outlook range, the market will give it the benefit of the doubt and re-rate it to those levels again?

I don't think so, right?

But the level of pessimism is also somewhat reflected in the implied discount to long-term averages when compared to the peers listed above, no?

For UNH, the discount is almost 18% below the long-term mean.

In CVS's (CVS) case, it's generally aligned with the long-term average, while in Elevance's (ELV) case, the implied discount is closer to 17%.

My point is that it does appear to me that the market has seen enough with the axe that fell on UNH and is not likely to batter it further. You get what I mean?

Buy or Sell UNH Stock?

And we actually see such a constructive consolidation taking place in UNH's price action, right?

As you can see, the $270 has been holding since late January/early February, which is since UNH reported the Q4 earnings.

And last week's bullish reversal candlestick supports my conviction that buyers are using the opportunity to add shares despite these persistent uncertainties.

Another plus point is that last year's bloodshed that saw sellers taking it right down to the $235 level appears to be well behind us.

There is little in UNH's print indicating that we are going back to those levels.

My interim price target is somewhere closer to the $370 zone.

Which was really where the market initially took the recovery to, before tumbling back down in early 2026.

Hence, we know that buyers are waiting to come back, but they just want to see a lot more clarity in the earnings projections.

That's okay. However, if UNH's inflection point for 2026 were to work out well, then by the time they print the results for Q2, Q3, and Q4, the best opportunities to get on board may no longer look as good.

Note that UNH is still expected to post pretty reasonable earnings growth in the next couple of years, as I indicated earlier.

While markedly below trend, it does corroborate my belief that the worst is over. And UNH's price action is showing that level of conviction too.

If you are still biding your time to get on board, I urge you not to wait for too long.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Micron: I Was Wrong, This Time Is Indeed Different (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Micron (MU) has defied expectations, so much so as to cause me to withdraw my prior bearishness. It is becoming increasingly clear that the AI story is far from being played out, even if the AI companies like MU are delivering unprecedented numbers. MU has seen both growth rates and profit margins turn hyperbolic, leading to a significant windfall for the balance sheet. I am upgrading the stock but continue to caution regarding the long-term overhead from historic cyclicality.

MU Stock Price

I last covered MU in January, where I downgraded the stock to a sell rating as I cautioned that cyclicality would be likely to come back over the long term, even if the near term is more exciting than ever. The stock has fallen slightly since.

MU is delivering incredible financial results that even I failed to anticipate. These are enough for at least a partial upgrade.

MU Stock Key Metrics

MU is a leading semiconductor company focused on creating memory chips. While the bulk of the AI trade thus far has focused on GPUs, memory has rapidly gained in importance as the growing complexity in AI use cases has made running AI more memory-intensive.

While the memory chip market has historically been highly cyclical and price competitive, the extreme shortage has enabled memory chipmakers such as MU to demonstrate unprecedented pricing power. This was seen in the company’s most recent financial results, with revenue jumping 75% sequentially and 196% YoY to $23.9 billion, demolishing guidance of $18.7 billion.

For reference, management notes expectations for both DRAM and NAND bit shipments to grow by around 20%. While MU has indeed been seeing a shift to a more premium product mix, this gives a pretty good idea for how much of a role pricing has had in driving the recent results.

MU has seen gross margins jump to an astonishing 75%, with GAAP operating margins rising to an even more incredible 68%.

For reference, the company’s highest GAAP operating margin on a full-year basis over the last decade was 49% in 2018.

The strong results have led to a financial windfall for the company, with its balance sheet swinging from $4.8 billion in net debt in the second quarter of 2025 to $6.6 billion of net cash. For better or worse, I expect that net cash position to grow as I find it unlikely that the company will be able to put cash to work on CapEx so quickly given physical and operational limitations.

Looking ahead, management guided the third quarter to see around $33.5 billion in revenue, representing 260% YoY growth and 40% QoQ growth. Given management’s high visibility over the next year, I expect the company to soundly crush that target.

On the conference call, management gave expectations for “both DRAM and NAND to remain tight beyond calendar 2026.” Management expects to spend “above $25 billion” in capital spending in fiscal 2026 but to be able to “fulfill only 50% to two-thirds of their demand in the medium term.” Management noted that recent contracts differed from those in prior years with far more favorable terms as far as commitments. They cited the “structural challenge of bringing on supply,” which actually is the opportunity as the company looks poised to benefit from incredible pricing power indefinitely.

Is MU Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, MU traded at just under 4x FY27e earnings. Most of the multiple compression has been due to analysts ramping up estimates to account for the rising demand, but some of it also has to do with recent volatility surrounding Alphabet’s (GOOGL) release of TurboQuant, which suggests more memory efficiency in AI applications. In regard to the latter, I actually share the view of bullish investors, namely, that the phenomenon known as Jevon’s Paradox suggests that more efficient memory may actually lead to increased memory usage.

Consensus estimates call for MU to generate incredible revenue growth through FY27. MU’s valuation of 2.5x FY27e sales is now below the 3.7x 5-year average.

First, let’s discuss why I am upgrading the stock from a sell to a neutral rating. My prior bearishness centered around my view that cyclicality would be inevitable. I again point out that while total assets have doubled since 2018, the company’s GAAP operating profit in FY25 was still 40% lower than that seen in 2018. The company’s current fundamentals have clearly taken off since last year, but my point is that history has shown that this is the kind of industry in which margins eventually come down over the long term.

I still retain that view, but it is clear that the high points of this cycle may end up far higher than anyone can expect. With agentic AI looking inevitable, I would not be surprised if consensus estimates end up proving too low for both FY27 and FY28, at the very least.

Yet with that in mind, I must answer why I am not upgrading this to a bullish rating, with the stock trading at a low-single-digit multiple of out-year earnings. To that, I must ask for forgiveness, as I will make a comparison which bullish investors might not take kindly. I view MU’s memory chip business as being very similar to that of the oil producer industry. Sure, MU has indeed demonstrated some technological innovation in their most recent products. That said, I see little reason to go against my long-held view that the memory chip market is one in which supply will eventually catch up with demand. The other operators in Samsung and SK hynix, together with MU, are highly incentivized to aggressively ramp up CapEx in order to fulfill more orders. Just as often occurs when oil prices spike, the natural order of business may eventually bring prices down. Again, I must make it clear that it may take quite some time before production ramps up to the point where the historical cyclicality can return in full force. However, with the history of GAAP operating margins often peaking and then plunging back to zero or even negative at what feels like every handful of years, I would expect MU stock to be constantly held back by the fears of cyclicality. Veteran investors know that cyclical stocks often are cheapest when their P/E multiples are highest and most expensive when their P/E multiples are lowest due to that phenomenon. I think there’s considerable risk that MU stock stays in a range of 2x to 3x earnings, which would put a lid on the potential upside and make it difficult to justify an outright bullish rating.

MU Stock Conclusion

I differ from the bears in my view that TurboQuant is unlikely to be a meaningfully negative driver for MU. If anything, I think it may only lead to accelerated demand, especially over the coming years. I expect MU to beat consensus estimates over the medium term, as there are indeed structural reasons holding back supply from catching up. That said, I do expect supply to eventually catch up, and this may put pressure on the upward potential of the stock. I am upgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

data.title: ARC Resources: Still Not Noticed By The Market
data.description:
The last article noted that the market could not wait to dump ARC Resources (AETUF) stock on the least bit of bad news. This despite the fact that the first quarter earnings reported noted only a minor setback in that some wells were not coming online as expected. So, management "took a timeout" to understand exactly what is going on. Despite the assessment that this situation did not affect long-term plans, the market could not exit the stock fast enough. Since then, there has been a pricing recovery back. Both the growth story and acquisition story here covered in the past remain unchanged. This is that rare stock that can grow, buy back shares, and pay a dividend. That part of the strategy also remains the same.

Stock Price

Despite the recent pullback, the stock still had a decent return from the last article.

(Note: This Is A Canadian Producer That Reports Using Canadian Dollars Unless Otherwise Noted. It Also Trades On The Toronto Stock Exchange Where Liquidity Might Be Better)

At the same time, the Quant System of the website has this stock as a sell complete with the warning shown above. You have to remember that the Quant System is basically historically based and that there is a recent stock price pullback despite the rise from the upsetting news (that the Quant System has likely factored in).

My own belief here is that since the original announcement that management just wanted to take time to understand the latest well results, then management will soon be announcing a return to normal. That should cause a quant system re-evaluation of the situation.

Furthermore, the price-earnings ratio is far too low for what is a growth story in the industry. This is an investment-grade (debt-rated) company. Those types of companies rarely get into serious trouble. Instead, they usually resolve issues on a prompt basis.

But that means an expectation of a price-earnings ratio near normal or average is a realistic expectation for a company like this.

Furthermore, the condensate produced by this company typically sells at a premium to light oil in Canada. That means that the latest rise in oil prices is very good news for a company that sells a product that is premium to the light oil benchmark.

In summary, this stock is a typically out-of-favor stock, as evidenced by the threat issued by the Quant System to do worse (and the sell rating). What was probably not taken into account was the announcement of evaluating unexpected results did not indicate a major problem. Instead, it indicated that an optimization process was needed to keep costs down. For a big expansion project, such things happen on a fairly routine basis. I therefore would expect some "good news" meaning that a return to normal would be a usual expectation. The perceived problem would therefore be resolved to change the future outlook of the company and the stock.

First Quarter

Also mentioned in the past was the effect of La Niña on natural gas prices. Natural gas prices are generally seasonally strong in the winter to begin with. The La Niña winter effectively "added an exclamation point" to that statement by helpfully contributing periodic arctic blasts that raised natural gas usage beyond expectations at the beginning of winter.

Now, when natural gas prices seasonally decline (due to nice Spring weather even in Canada), the Iranian situation is sending condensate prices higher in tandem with the skyrocketing oil prices. The premium situation is almost irrelevant compared to the margin expansion caused by higher liquids pricing towards the end of the first quarter.

Unlike many in the industry, this company should benefit from the stronger natural gas prices combined with the higher condensate prices. If the situation is seen (as many do) as a "license to print money," then a company with a premium product (like condensate) is going to "print even more money."

Factor in the fact that the production expansion project is beginning to produce more at an optimal time.

To me, in summary, I think the market (as shown by the price-earnings ratio) has the situation all wrong even after the roughly 20% price run-up from the last article. With still more growth projected by the company, a re-evaluation of the company's prospects resulting in a larger price-earnings ratio is not out of the question.

The average market price-earnings ratio is around 17 (give or take a big time) right now. This company has got to be at least average given its low costs, investment-grade rating, and future growth prospects. There are a lot worse companies valued at 17 times earnings than this one. Now let's see what happens.

Acquisitions Are A Possibility Too

The market almost never factors in the history that is here (and at other companies) of an occasional opportunistic acquisition that adds to the growth rate.

Long-time readers may remember that when Strathcona (STHRF) offered some acreage for sale to raise cash while engaging in another bidding battle, this company moved extremely fast to snap up that acreage. I honestly did not realize that this company could move that fast given its size.

The acquired acreage is shown above along with the accompanying infrastructure. That, of course, resulted in unplanned condensate production (and natural gas as well). Now management is in the process of improving the situation even more. The progress is shown above.

Bolt-on transactions like this one are often very low risk because the acquiring company is often very familiar with the acreage and neighboring operations. The low debt enabled a very fast-moving, all-cash offer. Even with the debt used to make the cash offer, the debt ratio is still considered low.

But the key here is that acquisitions like this add a few percentage points (all at once) to the long-term growth rate of the company. In addition to the immediate production increase (which was considered accretive), there are long-term improvements that add still more to the average growth rate.

Summary

This is one of those situations where either the cash rolls in or even more cash floods in. For a large company to continue to be as profitable as this one is a highly unusual situation. Additionally, this strong buy idea buys back stock, repays debt, and pays a dividend (all the while growing production in the process). This is a dream situation for much of the industry.

Yet that price-earnings ratio is significantly below average all because management decided they wanted to evaluate what was going on with the latest wells rather than sticking to the previously guided schedule. To me, that means there is way too much emphasis on quarterly results and not enough emphasis on the long-term picture.

Management never guided a materially worse change in the major expansion project underway. The first quarter should reinforce that everything is ongoing with a return to the "normal" expansion schedule. But that return will be marked by an adjustment somewhere for what was going on with some of those wells. That is really no big deal, and it is certainly not a reason for the stock price sag.

When I find good management like this that has a very profitable situation, I tend to hold onto the stock until the story changes materially or the stock gets way overpriced. I just do not find too many companies like this one.

Risks

The primary risk for this company is a severe and sustained cyclical downturn caused by a recession or worse. Sometimes, if they are bad enough, they make the premium that condensate receives disappear. That could make a downturn worse for this company than is the case for many in the industry.

There is also the possibility of a serious problem developing with the expansion acreage that causes severe delays or cost overruns. If the situation gets unexpectedly bad, the whole project could be cancelled. Right now, things seem to be pretty much "on track." But let us see how the first quarter report unfolds in a few weeks.

Canada and the United States have also been bargaining about trade. This bargaining involves threats of tariffs and sometimes worse things. This needs to be replaced with something permanent and constructive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cargojet Downgrade: Shielded, But Not Immune To Macro Pressure
data.description:
Cargojet (CGJTF, CJT:CA) is one of the companies that I like. As an enabler of express deliveries in Canada, the company is positioned well to benefit from growing e-commerce sales while its business includes some shielding against cost and demand fluctuations. However, since my last report, the stock has fallen 10.7%, underperforming the S&P 500’s 4.1% loss. In this report, I discuss the various shielding mechanisms that Cargojet has but also highlight a major reason why the company’s earnings may still substantially fluctuate, and I update my price target.

Even With Shield Mechanisms Cargojet Stock is Under Pressure

Cargojet has been facing some pressures for a year. The global trade war does not directly impact Cargojet’s activities between the US and Canada, and that is for the simple reason that most of the goods cross the borders by rail and road. However, the company is facing some challenges as the part of the business where the company provides aircraft, crew, maintenance, and insurance saw a shift from transatlantic flights to shorter flights to South America driven by capacity allocation being tweaked by ACMI customers on-demand changes in trade lanes. The result has been that the company’s airplanes in the ACMI network are flying fewer block hours, rendering a lower compensation. The elimination of the de minimis exemption also had a volume impact on the business in Canada. Cargojet has tried to reduce any negative impact by opening new routes.

We can now add a new pressure to that, and that is the war in Iran, which has resulted in higher oil prices and higher freight rates but also an uncertainty about economic growth prospects.

In the domestic network, the company has several mechanisms in play through minimum guarantees, fuel pass-through, and inflation corrections. So, even with higher energy prices increasing the cost of operations and potentially resulting in inflation increasing, there is some shielding. However, while minimum guarantees provide some comfort, a reduction in flight activity can hurt the business because a minimum guarantee does not guarantee that profits will not decline. It mostly guarantees that a level somewhere above break-even can be achieved even when demand for cargo operations is low.

In the ACMI business, the company is best shielded against variable cost fluctuations, but we note that softer economic growth may reduce demand for ACMI operations. On charters, Cargojet is more exposed. Last year the company opened more charter operations to offset softness elsewhere, and that is providing a bit of a headwind.

So, from an operational perspective, the company is generally shielded well, but its charter business has full oil price exposure, and reduced demand for activity on the domestic distribution network and ACMI due to lower economic growth prospects is something that even Cargojet cannot offset, and that is in part because the company cannot easily take out costs of the business because it generally already operates rather efficiently with some capacity to handle higher volumes.

However, GDP growth is also where things get more complicated. If the war were to end in 2-4 weeks from now with Brent oil at $100-$120, Solability calculated that the global GDP impact would be 1.26, but GDP in Canada would grow by 0.3% as the country benefits from energy exports. So, each part of the business has a different exposure to oil prices but also to GDP.

Cargojet Stock Still Has Appeal

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Cargojet, I have modeled in the pressures, and that is also when the relative strength becomes visible. Revenues are somewhat impacted, but I expect it to be less than a percent with a 3% EBITDA impact and an 8.3% to 11.1% impact on free cash flow depending on the year. Free cash flow shows a more disproportionate reduction driven by the fact that we are keeping CapEx assumptions unchanged. The company, however, does have the ability to alter the growth CapEx as needed. However, I do believe that even with the CapEx lever not being altered, the stock is attractive with 40% upside and an $83.48 price target.

Margins are expected to continue their way down, but free cash flow remains positive. The way the business is shielded, I don’t believe we will see free cash flow heading into negative territory due to the shielding mechanisms and CapEx allocation that can be adjusted. The cash balance looks somewhat horrible, but driven by the shielding that the company has on its lines of business, it can get away with operating at low cash levels and leverage against the higher side of the range that I believe is comfortable.

Conclusion: Cargojet Remains Attractive Even With Pressures

Cargojet is not fully shielded from macroeconomic pressures, but we note that revenue and EBITDA pressures are manageable, driven by the way the business has various mechanisms in place to pass through costs. Free cash flow pressure is somewhat bigger but also manageable through adjustment of allocation of growth CapEx. We also note that while global GDP may be under pressure if the war does not end soon, for Canada, the high-energy prices are actually providing a tailwind. For Cargojet, I am downgrading the stock from strong buy to buy to also keep in mind that there is uncertainty on how the situation will develop.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp: Digesting The Comerica Acquisition
data.description:
Introduction

Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) is the Ohio-based holding company that owns the Fifth Third Bank, with more than $200 billion in assets on its balance sheet. The bank operates over 1,100 full-service banking centers, while its investment advisory businesses have close to $700 billion in assets under care before taking the recent acquisition of Comerica into account.

Last year, I focused on the preferred stock issued by this bank, and now we see an increasing pressure on fixed-income securities. I wanted to take a look and see if the bank’s preferred securities are now offering an acceptable risk/reward ratio.

The Preferred Dividends Enjoy a Solid Coverage Ratio

During 2025, the bank recorded a total interest income of just over $9.9 billion, and although this represented a decrease of approximately 5% compared to the FY 2024 interest income, the interest expenses decreased at a faster pace, both in absolute numbers as well as expressed as a percentage. The almost $880 million decrease in the interest expenses resulted in a robust 6% increase in the net interest income, which jumped to just shy of $6 billion.

Although the net interest income is an important part of the bank’s ability to generate a strong earnings result, it is good to see its non-interest income is increasing as well. I’m mainly pleased with the continued growth in the fees related to the wealth and asset management division.

These elements resulted in a pre-provision and pre-tax income of just under $3.9 billion. And while the total amount of loan loss provisions has increased to the highest level in a few years' time, the $662 million remains very manageable, as it represents just 1/6 of the total pre-tax and pre-provision income. The total net income came in at $2.52 billion, which, of course, was more than sufficient to cover the $146 million in preferred dividends. This means the total net profit attributable to the common shareholders was approximately $2.38 billion, which represents an EPS of $3.56 based on the weighted average share count of just over 668 million shares.

While Fifth Third repurchased stock during 2025 to reduce its share count, the current amount of shares outstanding exceeds 900 million, as Fifth Third issued approximately 240 million new shares to the shareholders of Comerica to close the acquisition of Comerica (which occurred on February 1). The combination with Comerica should boost the consolidated earnings of Fifth Third and have a slight positive impact on the preferred dividend coverage ratio. As part of the all-stock deal, Fifth Third also converted $400 million in preferred stock that was issued by Comerica into a new series of its own common shares. The new Series M preferred stock is a fixed-rate, reset preferred issue and currently offers a preferred dividend coupon of 6.875%.

The share buyback program has been paused until the acquisition of Comerica was completed, and it will now be interesting to see if Fifth Third restarts these share repurchases. It has an active buyback authorization for over 90 million shares, which would allow the bank to retire 10% of its current share count.

Another Look at FITBO

A little while ago, I discussed the bank’s Series K non-cumulative preferred stock, which is trading with (FITBO) as its ticker symbol. This series of preferred capital was issued in 2019 and has been callable since 2024. But as this represents a very low cost of capital (this series of preferred stock has a preferred dividend coupon of 4.95% based on the $25 principal value), I doubt Fifth Third Bancorp is looking to retire these shares anytime soon.

The 4.95% preferred dividend represents a payment of just under $1.24 per preferred share per year, payable in four quarterly preferred dividend payments of just under 31 cents per share.

As you can see above, this series of preferred stock is trading at $19.06 right now, which implies the current yield is approximately 6.49%. And with the 5-year U.S. Treasury yield trading at close to 4%, this represents a mark-up of 250 bps to the risk-free interest rate.

When I’m ready to pull the trigger on buying the preferred stock, I’m planning on simply buying the issue that has the highest yield at that time.

Investment Thesis

I currently have no position in either the common or the preferred stock of Fifth Third Bancorp. I will continue to follow Fifth Third Bancorp as the bank appears to be well-managed, and perhaps there will be an opportunity to pick up stock when the dust settles after the acquisition of Comerica.

The share price recently came under pressure after pretty light guidance for Q1, which has an impact on the FY net interest income guidance as well (the midpoint was reduced from $4.2 billion to $4.1 billion). But given consensus estimates of a $4.90 EPS next year followed by $5.33 in 2028, there still is upside in the common stock as well, but perhaps we should consider 2026 to be a transition year wherein Fifth Third integrates the Comerica acquisition.

data.title: Broadcom Is Ready To Wake Up From Its Slumber
data.description:
Earlier this year, I reiterated my buy rating on Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) citing AI-fueled growth, strong guidance, and a valuation that had contracted. This was a bullish setup, but as you can see in the chart below, ultimately there have been some moderate declines seen since then. Broadcom reported their latest numbers last month, and with the semiconductor industry constantly advancing and shifting, I believe that it is time to provide readers with another update now. By the end of this article, I aim to provide a fresh investment thesis for the stock.

Below, it is shown that AI continues to drive growth for Broadcom. Both AI and company-wide growth accelerated for Q1 while adjusted EBITDA margin remained unchanged to show off solid profitability. Looking forward, guidance for Q2 is impressive, and targets for 2027 are ambitious. While there are some risks in the form of capacity constraints, the valuation slide is creating more and more opportunity for long term investors. Broadcom stock has undeniably been mostly dead money now for months, but at this point, I believe it could be ready to wake up again soon. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating.

Growth Continues To Reaccelerate

Let's start with a look at Broadcom's top line performance. For their FY2026 Q1, the company reported revenues of $19.311 billion, up 29% YoY. You can see in the above chart that this is another acceleration of growth. The inflection point that happened a few quarters ago is now all but confirmed, and that is encouraging after a steep growth slowdown seen in the year-ago quarter. Overall, the company's business trajectory now seems to be robust. We will talk about some potential supply constraint risks later in this article, but as of now activity has held up respectably. With the company having beat top line expectations by $171.52 million, Q1 was a quarter of nice outperformance.

Things are perhaps even more encouraging when you look at their AI specific revenue results. For Q1, Broadcom generated AI revenues totaling $8.4 billion, up 106% YoY. This is also an acceleration of growth from 74% in the previous quarter, and so there has been a strong rebound. The company cited "robust demand for custom AI accelerators and AI networking." While XPUs take most of the headlines, investors should not overlook their networking solutions. In my recent article on Nvidia (NVDA), it was shown that their networking business was actually growing even faster than their compute business in percentage terms, and so this area seems very lucrative.

Profitability & Returns

While growth is continuing to rebound, Broadcom has also stood its ground in terms of profitability for the most part. You can see above that EPS growth did slow a bit from the previous quarter to 28.12%, but they did still edge past estimates. Perhaps more importantly, their adjusted EBITDA margin stayed unchanged YoY. This is one of the best measures of core profitability out there for a company like Broadcom, and the fact that the metric has stood steady at 68% shows that profitability has remained in a strong position. We will look at their guidance in just a minute, but at this point, fears of networking or even chip commoditization seem to be unfounded.

With operating cash flows and FCFs being healthy, it is nice to see that Broadcom is continuing with its capital returns. While their dividend yield isn't the most generous right now, it should be noted that Seeking Alpha rates their dividend safety, growth, and consistency as A-, A+, and B, respectively. In addition to this reliable income, Broadcom announced that they had authorized a new $10 billion share buyback program that runs through to the end of the calendar year. Back in the previous fiscal year, the company bought back just $2.45 billion in stock, and so it seems that they are upping their capital returns overall. Their choice to increase buybacks rather than the dividend may signal that the stock is undervalued.

Outlook: Q2 & Beyond

Moving forward, Broadcom expects to generate $22 billion of revenue for their FY2026 Q2. This was above consensus estimates and implies YoY growth of 47% YoY. It will also be a steep acceleration of growth from what we saw in their Q1 and so it seems that the restrengthening in business momentum is set to continue.

For their AI semiconductor revenues in particular, they see revenues of $10.7 billion for their Q2. If this materializes, they will be looking at YoY growth of 143%, and of course that would also be a steep acceleration from their Q1. Looking further out, in their earnings call CEO Hock Tan expressed that they are forecasting 2027 AI chip revenue of more than $100 billion. He went on to say that they have "secured the supply chain required to achieve this." As of now, it looks as though AI-driven growth will continue for the foreseeable future.

As for profitability, they see the adjusted EBITDA margin being at around 68% for Q2. That would represent a modest improvement from the prior year period's 67%. This expansion should further keep commoditization fears at bay. At this point, core profitability is, in fact, set to move in the right direction for Broadcom. As a whole, I'm impressed with their Q2 guidance and their 2027 projection for AI chip sales.

Opportunities & Risks

Currently, there are strong indications that hyperscalers remain very interested in what Broadcom has to offer. Back in March, it was reported that Meta (META) is preparing to deploy four new generations of their custom AI chips by 2027. Plans for the MTIA 300, MTIA 400, MTIA 450, and MTIA 500 have been announced, and of course these will be developed with Broadcom. The expanding internal use and potential external use of these AI chips will mean a further boost to growth for the ASICs giant. Moving forward, trends towards custom chips show that the opportunity is continuing to expand for Broadcom.

At OFC 2026, Broadcom made it clear that innovation remains one of their most important competitive advantages. The most significant announcement was perhaps the release of their new 400G/lane optical DSP Taurus BCM83640. Vice president and general manager of Broadcom’s Physical Layer Products Division Vijay Janapaty stated that

Taurus, the industry's first 1.6T DSP based on 400G/lane I/O, doubles the throughput per lane to enable the next generation of 3.2T optical modules. Crucially, Taurus pushes the IMDD technology envelope into 400G/lane, further reducing power and advancing our roadmap of cost-optimized solutions for connectivity in AI and cloud networks.

This innovation is required to meet the increasing bandwidth needs of data centers, and so Broadcom's R&D efforts are paying off. In addition to Taurus, the company also showcased their 3.5D XDSiP, 800G AI NIC, and much more at OFC 2026. In my view, it is clear that the company's own innovation is giving them opportunities to grow further.

While it was mentioned in the outlook section that Broadcom has secured enough capacity for $100 billion of AI chip sales in 2027, limited capacity at TSMC (TSM) remains a potential risk to their growth prospects. Director of product marketing in Broadcom's Physical Layer Products division Natarajan Ramachandran stated, "We are seeing that TSMC is hitting (production capacity) limits." There are reportedly bottlenecks in the laser space and in printed circuit boards. Engagement lead times are also now quite extended. Therefore, while the $100 billion target for 2027 is strong, growth could be even stronger if TSMC did not have such bottlenecks. Capacity constraints are probably one of the main risks that semiconductor companies face today.

Valuation Looks Cheap

Since my previous article, Broadcom's forward P/E ratio has slowly slid from the mid thirties range to a current reading of 27.83. If it was a buying opportunity back then, it should be an even better buying opportunity now. I believe that is indeed the case for investors.

In today's article, many positive signs were shown for Broadcom's fundamentals. Top line growth continued to rebound to confirm an inflection point is in, and the EBITDA margin held steady for Q1. Moving forward, guidance for Q2 was more than solid, and the 2027 target for AI revenues is ambitious and believable at the same time. While TSMC bottlenecks may be a risk to growth, the company's evident innovation in networking solutions should be a factor in keeping the long term prospects of the company bright. As a whole, the current fundamentals and outlook seem favorable to me.

With that being the case, the continued slide in the valuation creates ever more opportunity for long-term investors, in my view. For a company that is seeing triple digits of AI growth and expecting margin expansion moving forward, a forward P/E of below 30 seems like a pretty good deal.

Concluding Remarks

As of now, it seems that Broadcom stock is in a good position to wake up from its months-long slumber. It is undeniable that it has acted like dead money for an extended period now. However, with growth accelerating, margins expected to expand, and the opportunities outweighing the risks, I believe the valuation contraction makes Broadcom quite irresistible at current levels. While the stock has seen losses of nearly 10% since I last reiterated my buy rating, I don't see many reasons why I shouldn't reiterate my bullish stance again today.

data.title: Zillow: Tide Shifting In Its Favor (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In a volatile market, the best trait for any investor is to be agile. This is not a time for passive stock investing and hoping that a rising tide will lift all boats: the choppy macroeconomy and investors' risk-off attitude mean that there will be a wider divergence between winning and losing stocks this year.

Zillow (Z) is a company on which I've changed my mind and done a 180-degree flip, based on the latest news. The real estate data giant remains a lightning rod for pessimism this year, with its stock down ~40% since the start of the year. That said, I believe the tide is turning in Zillow's favor, and that the stock has reached its near-term bottom.

I last wrote a neutral article on Zillow in February, when the stock was trading at $45 per share. Since then, two major factors have shifted in the company's favor. First, Compass (COMP) has dropped its longstanding lawsuit against Zillow, removing a major uncertainty in the company's near-term horizon. Second, Zillow's stock itself has continued to plummet (though not as much as Compass has) while the company announced a substantive new buyback program. With both of these items considered, I'm upgrading my viewpoint on Zillow to buy.

Compass is showing chinks in its armor

Let's first start with a discussion of how Zillow's battle against Compass has evolved. As a brief history for investors who are unfamiliar with the two companies' clash, Compass raised Zillow's ire by emphasizing its use of "pocket listings," which are private listings that Compass agents selectively market to a pool of qualified buyers, bypassing the MLS.

Zillow's bread and butter has long been to scour MLS data to show homebuyers the most complete picture of available inventory in the market. Compass has grown larger and larger via acquisitions (most recently swallowing Anywhere Real Estate, the parent company of Coldwell Banker and Century 21, to become the undisputed largest brokerage firm in the U.S.), so the growing use of pocket listings by a swelling number of Compass agents was (and remains) a threat to Zillow's business and the completeness of its listings. Zillow retaliated by banning certain Compass agent-controlled new listings, which Compass claimed was anticompetitive behavior.

In mid-March, Compass dropped its suit against Zillow, presumably allowing Zillow's current listing ban policy to move forward (which effectively penalizes home sellers for using Compass agents, in that listings can't "go wide" on Zillow). While I still also remain long on Compass and believe it to be undervalued relative to its gaining market share and its adjusted EBITDA profile, I think the loss of this suit is the beginning of a turning point in the Compass vs. Zillow battle, in the latter's favor.

Consider the fact that shares of Compass have dropped 33% this year (almost as deep as Zillow's losses), reversing much of last year's gains. Investors are growing increasingly concerned that the brokerage business is ripe for disruption by AI, especially as AI can help sellers prepare their homes for sale, optimize pricing, market their listings, and draft purchase contracts. As much as Compass emphasizes its tech-enabled brokerage, its bread-and-butter revenue stream is still classic agency fees (2.5-3.0% of each home sale, for representing one side of the transaction). Over time, as AI automates more of a broker's workflow, it's difficult not to see traditional fee percentages coming down.

Zillow, however, is insulated from this risk. Its principal strength is not having the dominant number of real estate agents in the U.S., but having the primary site that homebuyers go to when searching for a home. Zillow then is able to convert its enormous traffic into advertising revenue (from agents), as well as value-add commission revenue (from multifamily landlords advertising their vacancies, and mortgage/title companies offering their services to Zillow homebuyers).

In late March, Zillow hosted an AI summit to discuss how AI is pushing the real estate industry forward. Jeremy Wacksman, Zillow's CEO, emphasized that Zillow is the primary top-of-funnel source for homebuyers, and that 80% of real estate search traffic is routed through Zillow's sites. Per his remarks during the summit:

The question we most get asked by this room is really around the top of funnel. Who gets the search? Who gets the first click? How will that change? Well, the reality is, at Zillow, we've long been and believed it is much more than that first visit. In real estate, in fact, consumers have had thousands of options to choose from for finding a listing or answering that first question. They choose Zillow because we build the great product experiences that they want and need and tell their friends about. And you see that in our results. 235 million unique visitors come to Zillow Group sites and apps every month, and 80% of that traffic is direct to us. That is people opening the app on their phone, typing Zillow into the browser, telling their friends about Zillow.

That doesn't come from intercepting demand from a platform. That comes from building the best experience consistently so they choose us. Yes, we have this huge scale of audience, and we have this brand that they trust. But more important, the reason they choose us is we are a platform for them. We are a destination for their entire housing journey, not just where it starts and the first question you ask, but the place to go to take action, to take the next step, to move forward with confidence in a transaction. And that is our focus and has been our focus, make Zillow the best real estate experience end-to-end."

Zillow has an opportunity to deepen the monetization from its enormous traffic, and it could even release AI-powered agent services directly on its site to counteract traditional brokers. Meanwhile, Zillow's victory against the Compass suit showcases that the latter may be presiding over the decline of the traditional brokerage model.

Boosted buybacks and attractive valuation

Shifting away from Zillow's growth potential amid a changing real estate industry, we find that the stock is attractive not just in the near-term future, but against today's profitability levels as well.

At current share prices near $41, Zillow trades at a market cap of $9.74 billion. After we net off the $1.30 billion of cash and $364 million of debt on Zillow's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $8.80 billion.

Zillow is expecting "mid-teens total revenue growth" this year, which implies $2.98 billion in revenue if we assume this means 15% y/y growth (in line with Wall Street's current consensus projections for the top line this year). The company has also vaguely guided to "margin expansion" this year, which I assume to mean a ~26% adjusted EBITDA margin (+2 points y/y versus a 24% adjusted EBITDA margin in FY25, and in line with the company's current pace of y/y margin expansion in Q4). Using this set of assumptions gives us adjusted EBITDA of $775 million (+25% y/y).

This indicates Zillow's current valuation sits at just 11.4x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA - which I find to be quite attractive given Zillow's near-monopoly in U.S. real estate search and the potential to boost monetization as AI upends the existing brokerage industry.

We note as well that in early March, after the company's Q4 earnings update, Zillow increased the size of its buyback program by a whopping $1.25 billion, which is worth 13% of the company's current market cap. Not only is this expansive buyback fully fundable from the company's existing balance sheet cash, but this is also a direct vote of confidence from Zillow's management that the stock is undervalued and ripe to be bought.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks that we need to keep in mind. At least in the near term, the bulk of Zillow's revenue still comes from its Premier Agent business. This means that the current slow housing market, as well as any AI-driven reduction in brokerage fees, will be a headwind to Zillow as well until the company can further diversify its revenue streams.

That said, we think these risks are priced in at just ~11x forward adjusted EBITDA, especially given the company's large pending buybacks and recent legal victories that eliminate other risks. Stay long here and buy Zillow for a rebound.

data.title: My Top Dividend Bargains For April 2026
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Spring is here! This is the season when we spend more time in the beautiful outdoors. Gardening enthusiasts will head back to their backyards, ready to create wonders from the soil. The more seeds you sow, the merrier the garden becomes, and the more meaningful the harvest will be for you and your family. A single plant may produce enough to perhaps feed you once, but many plants together can fill the fridge with produce and feed your family throughout the season.

There is also reality. There could be infestations, imbalances in soil nutrient profile, the soil’s water retention capability, or unfavorable weather conditions, which could lead to losses in your garden and diminish overall returns. But even with these setbacks, a well-diversified garden with appropriate care can produce an abundant, dependable harvest.

Our Income Method follows the exact same logic.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we plant widely. We accumulate a large, diverse collection of income-producing holdings so that even if a few underperform or experience infestations through price volatility, economic shifts, or sector-specific headwinds, our portfolio continues to deliver a strong, steady harvest throughout the year.

As April begins, two holdings stand out as reliable income producers in our income garden, resilient, productive, and built to nourish your portfolio through all seasons.

Let’s dive in.

Pick #1: ET – Yield 7.1%

Not everything has been selling off amidst escalating tensions in the Middle East. To our subscribers, we outlined days before the beginning of the military operations against Iran that this involves a region quite critical for the global energy movement.

“The tension between the U.S. and Iran will influence oil prices more profoundly, as the Strait of Hormuz is the most critical oil chokepoint in the world, with 20 million barrels of oil per day passing through it.” – HDO Article, February 26th

The U.S. shale revolution gained momentum after 2005, significantly transforming the American energy landscape from a mindset of scarcity (as seen in the 1973 oil crisis) to one of abundance. The United States is currently the largest producer (and one of the largest exporters) of crude oil and natural gas.

Without a reliable supply of energy and the infrastructure to move it from extraction to processing, storage, and end users, a nation cannot achieve energy independence. This problem is clearly seen from the LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) shortage in India and the closure of a substantial number of gas stations in Laos and Cambodia. Energy security is national security, and robust infrastructure is essential to supporting the industry and protecting the economy against geopolitical shocks.

Energy Transfer LP (ET) is a highly diversified midstream company with well-balanced business segments. ET is one of the largest midstream operators in North America.

Note: ET is a Master Limited Partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 to shareholders.

ET’s 2025 Adj. EBITDA represents a well-balanced business model, equally distributed between the following core segments.

  • Crude Oil (18%) – Transports, stores, and markets crude oil through its pipelines and terminals.
  • NGL & Refined Products (26%) – Transports, stores, and fractionates NGL (Natural Gas Liquids) and refined fuels.
  • Midstream (20%) – Gathers, processes, and treats natural gas.
  • Natural Gas Interstate & Intrastate Pipelines & Storage (20%) – Transports and stores natural gas across assets located nationwide.
  • SUN, USAC & Other (16%) – Includes 100% ownership of Sunoco LP (SUN) fuel distribution and 39% ownership of USA Compression Partners (USAC) natural gas compression services.

Source

ET holds higher debt levels compared to other midstream companies, expecting 2026 leverage between 4.0 and 4.5x Adj. EBITDA. This is due to significant CapEx (~$5.0-$5.5 billion earmarked for 2026), the majority around natural gas and NGL projects. ET has greater involvement in last-mile delivery to data centers, and the company has over 6 billion cubic feet/day of contracted pipeline capacity with a weighted-average life of 18 years, expected to generate over $25 billion in revenues from transportation fees. ET supplies natural gas to over 185 power plants across the country and has signed agreements with Oracle to provide natural gas to three data centers.

ET expects 2026 Adj. EBITDA between $17.45 and $17.85 billion, and targets a 3-5% annual distribution growth rate over the long term. About 90% of ET’s Adj. EBITDA is linked to fee-based contracts, implying that the company is not a direct beneficiary of higher commodity prices but is well-positioned to bring its newer assets to full capacity due to higher overall demand for midstream services.

Pick #2: DFP – Yield 7.5%

Flaherty & Crumrine Dynamic Preferred and Income Fund (DFP) experienced a dramatic few weeks since the beginning of the armed conflict between the U.S. and Iran.

DFP holds publicly traded preferreds, mostly issued by banking, insurance, utility, and energy companies, with 70% of them based in the U.S., 10% in Canada, and the rest in the E.U. and the U.K. Source

What do they have to do with oil prices and the Middle East? Not much, but preferreds are sensitive to interest rates, and the market’s fear of the worst (rate hikes to combat higher inflation) is catching up on this relatively steady asset class. Notably, over 53% of DFP’s portfolio holdings are investment grade.

DFP operates with a 37% leverage at a borrowing cost of SOFR + 0.90%, and the CEF ended FY 2025 (November 30, 2025) with a weighted-average interest rate of 5.2%. This positions the fund for lower borrowing costs with every rate cut.

But what happens when rates rise?

Borrowing costs rise, straining the 89% of DFP’s portfolio that is fixed-to-float securities, meaning that a substantial portion of this fund’s holdings will experience a reset in their coupon at staggered intervals. Looking at DFP since the rate hikes began in 2022, its NAV and distributions experienced a decline, followed by a steady rise as we continued to navigate through a declining rate cycle.

The higher allocation to rate-reset-type securities has resulted in a growing top line for the CEF as rate hikes intensified. Despite this, higher borrowing costs weighed down on funds available for distribution.

In recent years, DFP's distributions have mostly been Qualified Dividends, making it highly efficient from a taxation standpoint. Wall Street's fear about rate hikes to combat inflation will yet again be an opportunity for investors to buy fixed income on the cheap. To make things appealing, DFP trades at an 8% discount to NAV, letting you buy into the fixed-income space at a bargain.

Conclusion

A successful harvest isn’t built from a single plant; it’s built on many. At High Dividend Opportunities, we apply the same principle to our portfolio. We invest across several sectors and asset classes, including fixed-income positions. Our comprehensive bond ladder ensures that every year, a steady stream of capital matures and returns fresh cash to reinvest into the best opportunities the market offers, whether it’s a bull run or a downturn.

Our Rule of 42 enables our portfolio to benefit from an abundant, reliable flow of dividend and interest income. This is how we stay hungry and keep growing, even if market sentiment is weak. This is the power of income investing.

What are you growing in your backyard this season?

data.title: Don't Put All Your REIT Eggs In One Basket
data.description:
I began my career in commercial real estate - not behind a desk, but in the field.

As a developer, I learned how to create value from the ground up. In the 1990s, I analyzed balance sheets and income statements for dozens of retailers, including Dollar General (DG), Advance Auto (AAP), CVS (CVS), PetSmart, and many others. That’s when I learned the single most important lesson in investing: that the cost of capital drives everything.

Like many investors, I made the mistake early on of concentrating too much capital in one partnership… one geography… one property sector…and one asset class.

And I paid the price.

That experience shaped my philosophy - and ultimately led me to real estate investment trusts (REITs), where diversification, liquidity, and transparency create a more durable wealth creation model.

Today, as I return to Seeking Alpha, I want to focus on something timely:

Why REITs deserve a bigger allocation today—and where to find the widest moats.

Data Centers: Mission-Critical Infrastructure

Data centers are the backbone of the digital economy, where AI, cloud, and enterprise demand are creating a surge in need for power, connectivity, and secure infrastructure. Wide Moat drivers for the sector include:

  • Switching costs are enormous
  • Power access is scarce
  • Development is complex and capital-intensive
  • Long-term enterprise tenants

I’ve been covering data centers for over a decade, starting with Digital Realty (DLR) that IPO’d in 2004, the CyrusOne spin from Cincinnati Bell, and the Equinix (EQIX) REIT conversion in January 2015.

At one time, I also covered QTS Realty, CoreSite, and DuPont Fabros, all of which were acquired: QTS was sold to Blackstone for $10 billion in 2021, CoreSite was sold to American Tower for $10.1 billion in 2021, and DuPont Fabros was sold to Digital Realty for $7.5 billion in 2017.

CyrusOne was also acquired by KKR and Global Infrastructure for $15 billion in March 2022 and was one of my best picks ever, returning around 500% over 9 years (IPO’d at $19 per share and taken out at $90.50 per share, a strong validation of the REIT model.

I’ve also watched Iron Mountain’s (IRM) transformation from a pure physical records storage company (warehouse full of paper documents) into a hybrid storage model powered by data centers and digital infrastructure.

In November 2020, I argued that shares would “mushroom,” recognizing the “sticky” customer base, suggesting 40% annualized total return potential. Since that article, shares have returned 35% annually, that’s an exceptional compounding rate - well into “elite” long‑term performance territory.

Looking back at data center REIT performance over the last decade (2016-2025), as shown below, data centers have generated annual returns of 14.5%, the second-best performance across all REIT categories.

And so far in 2026, Data Center REITs have generated superior returns (as shown below) because, as I explained previously, they sit at the center of the AI + cloud + digital infrastructure boom.

The biggest catalyst, of course, is AI compute demand, in which hyperscalers like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google are leasing space at record levels. This demand has, in turn, created “supply scarcity” in key data center markets like Northern VA, Dallas, and Santa Clara.

In addition, as I noted earlier, private capital has validated the sector, and other REITs such as Realty Income (O), Prologis (PLD), and COPT Defense Properties (CDP) are acquiring data center assets.

Of the three “pure play” data center REITs, Equinix offers the best value, now trading at a multiple of 25x P/AFFO. That’s not a bargain whatsoever, recognizing that this REIT, as well as Digital Realty and Iron Mountain, are all expected to grow by around 10% over the next few years.

Hopefully, like me, you jumped onto the train before leaving the station, having recognized that AI demand is accelerating faster than expected.

Industrial & Logistics: The Arteries of Commerce

Industrial real estate is no longer cyclical - it’s structural. E-commerce, supply chain resiliency, and reshoring trends continue to drive demand. Wide Moat drivers include:

  • Infill locations near major metros
  • High land constraints
  • Sticky tenants
  • Embedded rent growth

As a previous chart highlights, industrial REITs were the best-performing sector from 2016 to 2025, returning 16.2% annually. However, so far in 2026, Industrial REITs have underperformed, generating returns less than 1% YTD, which suggests there’s potential value that can be generated by owning shares within the mission-critical category.

My top picks in the sector include Prologis (PLD), EastGroup (EGP), STAG Industrial (STAG) – all differentiated by design.

Prologis, of course, has a scale advantage, perhaps the closest thing real estate has to a monopoly on global logistics infrastructure. The $170 billion (Enterprise Value) REIT owns 1.3 billion square feet of distribution facilities in the most supply-constrained markets on earth – LA, Inland Empire, NJ ports, Tokyo, London, and even near BMW’s US manufacturing plant in my hometown of Spartanburg, SC

Prologis owns land that can’t be replicated and demand is driven by e-commerce, AI supply chains, and onshoring/reshoring. The REIT isn’t just a warehouse landlord; it’s a logistics ecosystem that creates wealth with its development pipeline ($43 billion), data insights on supply chains, and value-added services (energy, automation, and data centers).

EastGroup is a uniquely-positioned RET that owns 560 high-quality, small-bay industrial properties in the fastest-growing US Sunbelt markets – a niche that’s quietly becoming one of the most durable in REIT-dom. The company focuses on infill industrial markets where there’s tenant demand (population + job growth) and infrastructure (near ports and highways).

Unlike Prologis, which has bigger boxes, EastGroup focuses on smaller tenants that include local distributors, service providers, and e-commerce last-mile users. These “flex-space” tenants move less frequently, depend on proximity, and have fewer alternatives, which creates higher retention and pricing power.

As a result, EastGroup consistently reports strong same-store NOI growth and double-digit leasing spreads. The development pipeline (50% of the portfolio) is also active, which is a key driver for internal growth without overpaying in the open market.

This Mississippi-based REIT doesn’t get the headlines like data centers or AI Plays, but over time it delivers steady dividend growth, consistent AFFO expansion, and lower volatility than many peers…a perfect “sleep well at night” REIT.

Finally, there’s STAG Industrial, another Industrial REIT that I was the first analyst to cover in November 2011. “…because of very little competition in these secondary markets, STAG is able to act like a “bigger fish in a small pond”.”

When I published that article, STAG had a market cap of less than $600 million, and over the course of 15 years, the company has grown to a market cap of over $7 billion, generating a total return of 645% (annualized 15% per year).

STAG’s business model is straightforward: own a simple, repeatable cash‑flow machine that compounds quietly over time. By focusing on secondary markets (across 40 states) and mostly single‑tenant warehouses (500+ buildings) STAG’s highly functional business model benefits from:

  • Lower acquisition costs vs. gateway markets
  • Less competition from institutions
  • Higher initial yields

The “real moat” for STAG is its acquisition engine in which the company purchases properties at attractive cap rates while reducing tenant and geographic concentration. In doing so, STAG has generated consistent and predictable dividend growth, while also reducing the payout ratio (which enhances dividend safety).

In terms of valuation, Prologis is now trading around Fair Value with a P/AFFO multiple of 28x P/AFFO and a dividend yield of 3.4%. Analysts forecast growth of 8% this year and 9% in 2027, which suggests dividend growth is likely.

My suggestion here is to wait for a pullback and consider EastGroup instead; shares are trading at 27x (normal value is 29x) with a dividend yield of 3.3%. Analysts are forecasting 10% growth this year and 7% in 2027, translating into a total return estimate of 15% to 20% over the next 12 months.

STAG is trading at 17x, a tad below the company’s normal multiple of 18x. The dividend yield is 4.2%, and growth is forecasted at a modest 2% this year and 6% in 2027. I would look to own STAG if shares dip below 5% or more, which makes EastGroup my top pick in the Industrial sector.

Net Lease: Durable Income in an Uncertain World

When volatility rises, predictability becomes priceless, which is why net lease REITs deserve a place setting as they deliver stable, bond-like income with equity upside. The “wide moat” drivers for the property sector include:

  • Long duration lease contracts (15 to 20 years, plus options)
  • Tenants are responsible for expenses (taxes, insurance, maintenance)
  • Diversified tenant and geographic mix
  • Strong supply of products (sale-leasebacks, build-to-suits, etc.)

My top “sleep well at night” REITs in the net lease sector include Realty Income (O), Agree Realty (ADC), and Essential Properties (EPRT).

Many of you know that I’ve been covering Realty Income for over 15 years here on Seeking Alpha and I’ve been an investor in the business for over twenty years. I’ve watched the moat get wider as Realty Income has used its strong balance sheet and scale to deliver consistent and [predictable dividend growth.

When I first started on Realty Income, the company had just over 2,500 properties with a market cap of around $7 billion and today the platform boasts a market cap of $40 billion with a portfolio of over 15,000 properties (in all 5 states and Europe).

Realty Income has also moved beyond its legacy business model of land-lording for fast-food chains (like Taco Bell) to target casinos, data centers, grocery stores, warehouses, and convenience stores.

Meanwhile, Agree Realty has also become a more disciplined capital allocator by targeting fungible, investment-grade tenants and avoiding bookstores (e.g., Borders Books, which was once a customer) and Topgolf properties.

Credit to CEO, Joey Agree, who has exercised financial discipline to improve Agree’s balance sheet so the company can become more competitive by lowering its cost of capital to generate steady profits and dividend growth.

Essential Properties is also one of my favorite net lease REITs. The company began trading in June 2018, and as I explained in an article (back in 2018), “it’s becoming increasingly evident that M&A is likely in the Net Lease sector, and it is only a matter of time before consolidation transforms the top players into the next Wells Fargo and Bank of America in the Net Lease sector.”

EPRT has certainly become a contender in the net lease space, by focusing on middle-market businesses that are hard to disrupt online (car washes, medical, QSRs, etc..) and are more “needs‑based” (not discretionary retail). EPRT seeks to acquire properties at higher cap rates (than O and ADC) so the company achieves better investment spreads that drive AFFO per share growth.

In terms of valuation, Realty Income is trading at 14.4x P/AFFO (the normal is 17x), with a dividend yield of 5.2% (the company has increased its dividend for 31 consecutive years).

Given the size of the denominator ($88 billion EV) Realty Income has to work harder to grow the business (guided $8 billion in investments this year) which is one reason it has recently secured JV deals with Apollo and GIC.

Analysts are forecasting 3% growth in 2026 and 2027, and while that’s less than we’ve seen over the company’s history (5%-ish), I’m attracted to the 20% annual return made up of price appreciation (12%), dividend yield (5.2%), and growth (3%).

Agree is also attractive, with a P/AFFO multiple of 17.5x (normal is 19x) and dividend yield of 4.1%. Analysts expect 5% growth this year and next, which suggests the Michigan-based landlord's shares could also return around 20% over the next 12 months (4.1% + 5% + 10%).

EPRT has pulled back to a multiple of 17x (normal is 18x) and while the dividend yield of 4.0% is lower than O and ADC, the payout ratio is lower (around 65%).

EPRT provided 2026 AFFO per share guidance of $1.99 to $2.04, which implies a growth rate of about 7% at the midpoint (higher than O and ADC). I forecast shares could return 15% to 20% over the next 12 months.

Specialty REITs: Where the Moats Get Wider

This is where inefficiencies create opportunity.

Specialty REITs often operate in niche markets with limited competition, high barriers, and unique pricing power. I’ll highlight a few of my favorites and why they deserve shelf space within an intelligent REIT portfolio.

I like billboards, and I consider these highly regulated assets to be monopolies in plain sight (due to limited supply). Warren Buffett obviously agrees with me because his company Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) took a position last year worth around $140 million.

I own a few parcels of land leased to Lamar, and in my town, in which the REIT operates like a local monopoly, because they have the best locations. Although shares have pulled back to around 15.3x P/AFFO (normal is 15x) I would wait on a better entry point (14x is idea). The dividend yield is around 5% with growth estimates of 5% this year and next.

Cold storage is one of the most misunderstood sectors right now - and mispriced – due in large part to high construction and operating costs and oversupply. However, it’s due to complexity and oversupply that shares in Americold Realty (COLD) and Lineage (LINE) have been beaten down.

Of these two, I like AmeriCold given its higher 8% yield (LINE’s yield is 6.4%) and lower multiple (COLD trades at 8.3x and LINE trades at 10.1x). I caution you, however, that it may take a few more innings to see shares move up until capacity catches up with demand and utilization tightens.

I’m sure you’ve all heard the news that President Trump is looking to ban institutional investors from the single-family rental sector. I recently wrote an OpEd on the subject in which I explained that

“If policymakers want to improve affordability, the policy prescription is straightforward: increase supply. That means streamlining zoning and permitting, reducing regulatory barriers and encouraging private capital formation.”

Simply put, I believe REITs should be carved out of the proposed legislation because “from both a market and policy standpoint, it is unlikely to improve affordability - and could ultimately exacerbate the housing shortage investors are already pricing in.”

The two larger players in the specialty residential sector are Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH). Both are cheap, INVH trades at 15.7x (normal is 24x) and AMH trades at 17x (normal is 25x).

Keep in mind, headline risk is high, as the media narrative (Wall Street vs. Main Street) has created multiple compressions. I find it highly unlikely that policymakers will force a liquidation and disrupt current lease structures, as that would destabilize housing, which is politically risky.

Own shares now (dividend yield around 4.5%) and wait it out, I think the worst is already priced in.

Manufactured Housing may be the most durable moat in all of REIT-dom because of (1) extremely low turnover (sticky tenants), (2) high demand (silver tsunami), and (3) limited supply (zoning restrictions). Also, the so-called “affordability crisis” has created strong structural demand.

Within this sub-sector, I like Equity LifeStyle (ELS), which trades at 24.2x P/AFFO (normal is 30x) and offers a dividend yield of 3.4%. Analysts forecast growth of around 5%, and I’ve targeted shares to return around 20% over the next 12 months.

Finally, I want to highlight the casino REIT sector, which is also a sub-category of the Net Lease REIT sector.

Casinos are location‑dependent, capital‑intensive assets in which the licenses are hard to obtain. Operators invest billions into properties, and moving isn’t an option, which makes tenants highly committed and low risk to vacate.

Given the slowdown in Las Vegas and more recent activism at Caesars, VICI's shares have pulled back to 11.5x P/AFFO (the normal is 15.3x). While I can’t argue with the softness we’re seeing in Las Vegas, the future of “Sin City” looks bright as Allegiant Stadium will officially be home to the 2029 Super Bowl.

I’m happy to hold onto VICI’s 6.5% dividend and 3% growth until shares reprice, which could result in returns in excess of 20% during the next 12 months.

Final Thoughts: Build the Moat, Then Allocate Capital

REITs are not a monolith. They are a collection of businesses - some average, some exceptional and the key is to identify durable cash flows, strong management teams, competitive advantages, and intelligent capital allocation. As much as I love REITs, I would not recommend putting all your eggs in one basket.

But today…I believe it’s time to overweight REITs.

Because when you combine:

  • Attractive valuations
  • Reliable income
  • Structural demand drivers

…you get an asset class that deserves a larger seat at the table.

I’m excited to be writing again on Seeking Alpha and reconnecting with this community.

As always, I welcome your thoughts, ideas, and feedback.

Happy Easter!

Author's Note: Brad Thomas is a Wall Street writer, which means he's not always right with his predictions or recommendations. Since that also applies to his grammar, please excuse any typos you may find. Also, this article is free: Written and distributed only to assist in research while providing a forum for second-level thinking.

data.title: A Hot CPI Report Could Force A Major Market Repricing
data.description:
The March CPI is expected to be a real barn burner. It is no mystery at this point as to why WTI Crude oil (CL1:COM) and gasoline prices are surging. The part with less clarity is how hot it will be and what it means for markets.

Analysts forecast the March headline CPI to rise 0.9% month over month, up from 0.3%. Headline CPI is expected to rise to 3.3% year-over-year. Even core CPI isn't expected to be great, rising to 0.3% m/m up from 0.2% in February, while rising 2.7% y/y from 2.5% y/y.

Gasoline Prices Have Surged

According to the EIA, all grades of conventional gasoline rose to $3.914 by March 31, from $2.89 in late February, a massive jump of about 35%. This is important because gasoline had a 2.812% weighting in the CPI report in February. At that kind of weight, gasoline doesn’t need to do much to move the index. A 15% increase adds roughly 0.4% to CPI, and depending on how the monthly average plays out, the contribution could approach 0.5%–0.6%. More importantly, if these effects are not fully realized in March and gasoline prices remain elevated into April, the inflation impulse could extend further.

Additionally, gasoline prices are now trading near the upper end of their historical range seen during other inflationary regimes, such as the 2007 to 2008 period and 2022, and may be consistent with a longer-term inflationary regime if they do not come down quickly. Additionally, the higher gas prices between 2010 and 2016 certainly had a knock-on effect on overall economic growth.

The online prediction website Kalshi, which has in the past been a reliable source for CPI reports, is even pricing in a 0.9% m/m increase in inflation for March and 3.3% on a year-over-year basis, in line with analysts' estimates. Core CPI is expected to come in at 0.3% m/m and rise to 2.7%, also in line with analysts' estimates. The CPI swaps market is also pricing in a similar rise in the market y/y inflation rate, from 3.3% in March to around 3.9% in April.

No Rate Cuts

Due to the spike in energy prices and the inflation it is expected to bring, markets are already repricing the path of monetary policy. Currently, Fed Fund Futures are trading at 3.63%, effectively pricing out any rate cuts.

But plenty of questions remain about how things will unfold in the coming weeks and how quickly higher oil prices will begin to feed through the rest of the U.S. economy. Most regional Federal survey data points did not show a meaningful jump in their prices paid indexes in March, but the ISM Manufacturing Index did see its prices paid index jump to 78.3, its highest reading since the summer of 2022. The ISM services report will be released on Monday, and the price paid index could add some more color to inflationary pressures across the economy. Clearly, though, the sharp rise in the manufacturing sector suggests the rising costs are filtering through to the economy quickly. Let us not forget that prior to the rise in oil prices, metals such as copper, gold, platinum, palladium, and silver had also been rising, suggesting future inflationary pressures.

Whether the CPI report comes in hotter than expected is a strong possibility, given how quickly oil prices have risen. Simply given the rise in gasoline alone, the month-over-month reading is going to be firm; it just depends on how hot, and, when factoring in the other parts of the energy complex, which altogether have a weighting of more than 6% in the index, the impacts could be much more pronounced. Again, how hot it gets will depend on how quickly these higher energy costs feed through to the rest of the economy.

The market will be very quick to reprice the risk around the Fed having to act and push rates higher, even if there is a new Fed chair. It will be hard for a new Fed chair to cut rates if the rest of the committee is not in alignment. Simply looking through rising energy prices would also be a big mistake.

Obviously, if CPI comes in cooler, the market can rejoice, rates can fall, and the risk-on theme can play for a little bit longer.
